title: Working with the part that cannot trust its own perceptions
author: Kalesh

metaDescription:
The part that won't believe what your own eyes saw 

 I remember sitting across from my therapist, describing something that had happened the night before...
htmlContent:
The part that won't believe what your own eyes saw

I remember sitting across from my therapist, describing something that had happened the night before. A small thing, really. My partner had said something with a certain edge in their voice, and I felt that familiar drop in my stomach. The cold flush. The sudden distance from my own body.

"That sounds really hard," she said. And I immediately thought: maybe I'm exaggerating. Maybe I imagined the edge. Maybe I'm too sensitive. Maybe I'm the problem.

This is what I mean when I talk about the part that cannot trust its own perceptions. It's not just doubt. It's a whole system inside you that actively rewrites what happened, gaslights you from within, and makes you question whether your own eyes and ears and gut can be believed. Right?

Here's the thing. This part didn't appear out of nowhere. It was forged in the fires of a childhood where your perceptions were consistently invalidated. Where you said "that hurt" and were told "you're too sensitive." Where you saw something scary and were told "you're imagining things." Where your body screamed danger and the adults around you insisted everything was fine.

So the part learned. It adapted. It became the internalized voice of those adults, protecting you from the unbearable pain of being the only one who saw what was real. Because if you're the only one who sees the truth, you're either crazy or alone. And both of those are terrifying for a child.

Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma lives in the body's incomplete responses. But what about when the trauma includes having your own body's signals invalidated? That's a different kind of wound. It's not just that you couldn't fight or flee. It's that your own nervous system's warnings were treated as false alarms so consistently that you stopped trusting the fire alarm itself.

Stay with me here. This is the part that, even now as an adult, will watch you have a clear reaction to something and then immediately step in and say "but what if you're wrong?" It will take the evidence of your senses and hold it up to a light, searching for the flaw. It will find reasons to dismiss your own experience. It will protect you from the risk of being wrong by making you never quite sure you're right.

And the cost is enormous. You can't make decisions confidently. You can't trust your gut in relationships. You second-guess every boundary you try to set. You stay in situations that are bad for you because the part whispers "maybe it's not that bad, maybe you're overreacting."

For understanding how trauma fragments the self, a popular choice is Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors by Janina Fisher. She writes extensively about how these parts develop and how to work with them compassionately.

Why your nervous system learned to doubt itself

Let me be clear about something. This part is not your enemy. It's not a defect. It's not proof that you're broken. It's a survival strategy that worked. It kept you safe in an environment where being wrong about your perceptions was dangerous.

Think about it. If you grew up with a parent who would rage if you accused them of being angry, the part learned to check and double-check before making any claim about their emotional state. If you grew up being punished for crying, the part learned to question whether you were actually hurt enough to justify tears. If you grew up with someone who denied reality in obvious ways, the part learned to distrust all reality-checking.

Dan Siegel, the psychiatrist who developed interpersonal neurobiology, talks about how children need "attunement" from caregivers. They need someone to accurately read their emotional state and respond appropriately. When that doesn't happen, the child's brain doesn't develop a stable sense of internal reality. The neural pathways for self-perception get wired with doubt built in.

So this part isn't stupid. It's not malicious. It's doing a job it was hired for decades ago. The problem is, the job description has changed. You're not a child anymore. The people who invalidated you are probably not even in your life anymore. But the part is still working the old shift, still running the old protocol, still questioning every signal your body sends.

I notice this in my own life all the time. I'll be in a conversation with someone I trust completely, and they'll say something that lands wrong. My body reacts. My chest tightens. My throat closes. And then the part kicks in: "Are you sure? Maybe they didn't mean it that way. Maybe you're being defensive. Maybe you should just let it go."

And sometimes I do let it go. And sometimes that's appropriate. But sometimes I don't let it go because the part shuts down my access to my own truth. And then I spend the rest of the day feeling off, disconnected, not quite in my body, not quite sure what happened.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook. It has exercises specifically for rebuilding trust in your own perceptions and reactions.

How to start talking to the part that doubts

I want to offer you something practical. Not a cure, not a fix, but a way to start relating differently to this part. Because the goal isn't to get rid of it. The goal is to change your relationship with it.

First, notice when the part is active. What does it feel like in your body? For me, it's a tightening in my forehead and a slight dissociation from my physical sensations. For you, it might be a hollow feeling in your chest, or a sudden fatigue, or a sense of unreality. Just notice. Don't try to change it. Don't argue with it. Just observe.

Second, get curious. Instead of believing the part's content (you're wrong, you're overreacting, you can't trust yourself), get curious about the part itself. Ask it: What are you trying to protect me from? What would happen if I trusted my perception right now? What's the worst case scenario you're trying to prevent?

I do this in my own therapy. I'll feel the doubt rising, and instead of getting hooked by it, I'll turn toward it with curiosity. And often what I find is that the part is terrified. It's terrified that if I trust my perception and act on it, I'll be rejected. I'll be abandoned. I'll be told I'm wrong and then left alone with that shame. The part is trying to prevent that pain by making me never take the risk of being wrong.

Third, offer gratitude. I know this sounds weird. But this part worked hard for you. It kept you safe in impossible circumstances. You can thank it for its service while also letting it know that things are different now. You can say something like: "I see you. I know you're trying to protect me. Thank you for keeping me safe all those years. But I'm an adult now, and I can handle being wrong sometimes. I can handle someone disagreeing with my perception. I can survive that."

Richard Schwartz, who developed Internal Family Systems, calls this "unblending" from a part. You separate your core Self from the part so you can relate to it with compassion rather than being consumed by it.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents. It helps explain why you learned to doubt your own reality in the first place.

The slow work of rebuilding trust with your own senses

Here's where it gets uncomfortable. Rebuilding trust with your perceptions is not a one-time decision. It's not something you can affirm your way into. It's a practice, and it's a messy one.

You're going to get it wrong sometimes. You're going to trust a perception that turns out to be distorted by your trauma history. You're going to act on a gut feeling that was actually old fear, not current danger. And the part that doubts will use those moments as evidence that it was right all along.

That's okay. That's part of the process. The goal is not perfect perception. The goal is a relationship with yourself where your perceptions are taken seriously, considered, and given weight. Not blindly followed. Not automatically dismissed. Given a seat at the table.

I think of it like calibrating a compass. You don't just decide the compass is correct and follow it everywhere. You check it against landmarks. You notice when it seems off. You adjust. But you also don't throw the compass away because it led you astray once. You keep working with it.

Your perceptions are like that. They're not infallible. But they're also not worthless. They're data. They're information from your nervous system about what's happening in and around you. And you can learn to work with that data skillfully.

One practice that helps me is what I call "the pause and the check." When I notice a perception arising, I pause. I take a breath. I check in with my body. I ask: What am I actually sensing right now? Not what story am I telling about it, but what are the raw sensations? Tightness here. Heat there. A sense of contraction. And then I ask: What's the most generous interpretation of this data that still honors it?

Not the most critical interpretation. Not the most paranoid one. The most generous one that still takes the data seriously. Sometimes that means saying: "My body is telling me something is off. I don't know what it is yet. But I'm going to pay attention and gather more information before I dismiss it."

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery by Judith Herman. She writes about how trauma survivors need to rebuild trust in their own reality as part of healing.

What it looks like when this part starts to relax

I can't tell you this part ever fully goes away. I don't think it does, for most of us. The doubt is too deeply wired. The childhood too formative. The invalidation too constant.

But something shifts. The part gets quieter. It takes up less space. You can feel it coming and you can choose how to respond rather than being run by it automatically.

I notice it in small moments now. Someone says something that lands wrong, and instead of immediately doubting myself, I pause. I feel the reaction in my body. I acknowledge it. I might even say something like "I need a minute to process that." And then I decide what to do with the information.

Sometimes I still don't trust it. Sometimes I still second-guess. But the gap between the perception and the doubt has widened. There's space there. And in that space, I can breathe. I can choose. I can be a grown-up with my own experience rather than a child waiting for someone else to tell me what's real.

That's the work. It's not glamorous. It's not a breakthrough moment where suddenly you trust everything your body tells you. It's the slow, boring, daily practice of noticing, pausing, checking, and choosing. Over and over. For years.

And one day you realize you've been trusting yourself more than you used to. You've been making decisions without running them through the committee of doubt first. You've been setting boundaries without needing three people to confirm you're not being unreasonable.

Not perfectly. Not all the time. But more.

And that's enough. That's real progress. That's the part learning, slowly, that it can rest. That you've got this. That you can handle being wrong sometimes, and you can handle being right, and either way, you'll still be here, still breathing, still trying.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors - Janina Fisher's work on understanding how trauma creates internal parts
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook - Practical exercises for rebuilding self-trust and managing symptoms
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents - Understanding how childhood invalidation shapes adult perception
  • Trauma and Recovery - Judith Herman's foundational text on the trauma recovery process
